Setup & Data Check

What are we sizing — how was each piece estimated, and was the data good enough?

Goal

size that maximizes profit

new-patient basis

How each piece was estimated — and was the data good enough?

The Marketeligible prescribers
Counted directly from prescription claims. No estimation needed — this is measured.
All checks met
covers about 95% of the class prescriber-level detail fresh (May 2026) data-fitness detail ›
Targets & Call Plansegments, frequency
Provided by the Product team — segments and call frequency, used as given. Not re-derived here.
Provided input
Rep Capacitycalls a rep can make
Workload model from field data. Preferred method kept despite a data gap — see note.
Override — logged
Override: we used the preferred capacity model even though we only have team-level activity data, not rep-level. Reason: the rep-level feed was delayed; it will refresh next cycle.logged by the analyst · May 2026
Sales Responselift per call
Borrowed from comparable products. We wanted to model this product's own call-and-response history — but that data was too thin, so we fell back to comparable products.
Borrowed — low confidence
Fell back: no usable own-response history yet (need at least 2 cycles; have none). The market is also highly concentrated, which makes a borrowed curve less reliable. Confidence is capped until this is grounded.
The Economics$ per Rx, cost per rep
Standard unit-economics build. Cost is actuals; revenue and profit per prescription use forecast assumptions.
Modeled — forecast

What we know that the numbers don't capture

Market context — sourced read-only from the research brief
  • Prescribing is concentrated in a small number of large accounts — a geography-based territory carve may be the wrong model here. (research brief, May 2026)
  • A competitor is expected to expand in the second half — not reflected in this run. (research brief, May 2026)
  • The field team reorganized last quarter, so capacity assumptions may lag reality. (research brief, May 2026)

Read-only echo of the research brief — provenanced there, not edited here.

⚠ One input needs attention: Sales Response is borrowed, not measured. The size is still usable, but its confidence is capped — and this flag follows through to every screen it touches.

The Market

How many prescribers are there, and how many are worth calling on?

counted from prescription claims · all checks met
→ This chapter
how many prescribers
The market
~12,400
Worth calling on
8,900 (72%)

this is where the chain begins — every later number builds on the size of the market

About 12,400 prescribers write in this class. Of those, 8,900 (72%) are worth calling on — they account for the great majority of prescribing.

Who's in the market ● measured

Counted directly from prescription claims — not estimated.

Writes in this class~12,400
Worth calling on8,900 (72%)

The other 28% write so little that a call is unlikely to pay for itself. They stay on the map but off the call plan.

~95%

May 2026

modeled

Targets & Call Plan

Who do we call, and how often?

provided by the Product team · used as given
The market
~12,400
We'll call on
8,900
→ This chapter
about 8 calls / yr

how the call plan turns "who to call" into "how much effort each one needs"

Targets get about 8 calls a year on average, weighted toward higher-value prescribers. This plan came from the Product team and is used as given — we did not re-derive it.

Call frequency by segment provided input

Higher-value prescribers earn more visits. These tiers were set by the Product team.

Tier 1 — highest value~12 calls / yr
Tier 2 — mid value~8 calls / yr
Tier 3 — lower value~4 calls / yr

The assumptions behind this plan

Provided as given; shown here for traceability.

Target list completenessall targets accounted for
complete
Frequency by segmentset by the Product team
3 tiers
Blended average frequencyacross all targets
~8 / yr

Rep Capacity

What can one rep actually cover in a year?

workload model from field data · override logged
The market
~12,400
We'll call on
8,900
Calls / yr each target
~8
→ This chapter
~620 calls / rep / yr

how many quality calls one rep makes a year, after travel and admin — this divides into the workload to give the size

After travel, admin, and no-shows, one rep makes about 620 quality calls a year. We kept the preferred workload model even though one input had a gap — the override is on the record.

From a full year down to selling time ◐ modeled

Each step strips out time a rep can't spend in front of a prescriber.

Working days a year~230
Selling days (less admin, travel)~190
Quality calls a year~620

✎ Adjust assumptions
⚠ Treat this curve as a starting estimate, not a measured fact. Prescribing in this market is concentrated in a small number of large accounts, and we don't yet have this product's own call-and-response history — so the shape is borrowed from comparable products, not measured here. The size it produces is usable, but its confidence is capped until we ground it. → Ground it: vacant-territory comparison → Use this cycle's actual results next time → Or proceed, with this caveat on the record

The Economics

What's a prescription worth, and what does a rep cost?

standard unit-economics build · uses forecast assumptions
Calls / rep / yr
~620
Lift per call
from the curve
→ This chapter
~$1,150 / Rx · ~$310k / rep

this turns added prescriptions into dollars — and dollars into the profit-max size

Each new-patient prescription is worth about $1,150 in profit over a 3-year view; a fully-loaded rep costs about $310k a year. Cost is actuals; the per-Rx value uses forecast assumptions.

What a prescription is worth ◐ modeled

Built up from price, persistence, and a 3-year, new-patient view.

Net revenue per filled prescriptionafter discounts and rebates~$1,950
×Months a new patient stays on therapy3-year, new-patient viewpersistence
−Cost of goods and to-serveper prescription~$800
= Profit per prescription~$1,150

A profit figure, never labeled revenue. Carries a 3-year, new-patient basis everywhere it appears.

What a rep costs

Cost is actuals — the firmest input on this screen.

Salary, benefits, car, samplesfully loaded, per year
~$310k
Net revenue per Rxafter discounts/rebates · forecast
~$1,950
Profit per Rx3-year, new-patient view · forecast
~$1,150

← Sales Response step 6 of 8 · the economics are set — next, the recommended size and why The Recommendation →

The Recommendation

What's the right field size — and why there?

The market
~12,400
We'll call on
8,900
Calls / rep / yr
~620
Profit / Rx
~$1,150
→ Right size
132–171 (148 best)

the chain so far — every number above is clickable to see how it was built

Any size from roughly 132 to 171 reps earns about the same profit.

The flat zone is wide — so the size itself is not the sensitive choice here. The response estimate is. Because the sales-response curve is borrowed from comparable products, the edges of this range are soft (read them as "roughly 130–170," not exact).
◐ capped confidence — profit inherits the borrowed sales-response curve
★ 148 — the profit-max point. The single size that earns the most, before the last rep stops paying their way.
132 — same profit, lowest cost. The low end of the flat zone; the tool's tie-break default when profit is flat.
fewer reps more reps 132 ★148 flat zone

profit rises, then flattens across the shaded zone — the curve, not a single point

⚠ Why the answer is a range, not a point. Past about 132 reps, each additional rep adds almost no profit — that's the wide flat top of the curve. So 132 to 171 all land near the same profit; 148 is the precise peak, and the lower end of the range trades a little profit for meaningfully lower cost. The honest uncertainty here is the borrowed response curve, not the choice of size within the range.

What this size delivers (figures shown at the 148 profit-max point)

prescribers we'll call on (72%)

~$214M
net sales captured

~$92M
after paying for the field force

about break-even
what the final rep adds vs. their cost
Expansion return
~1.4×
profit per $ on the reps we're adding

Reality Check

Can we actually staff it? — the size only works if it splits into territories a real person can cover.

the market
12,400
we'll call on
8,900
calls / rep / yr
620
the size (flat zone roughly 132–171)
148 reps
staffable?
workable · 3 to watch

the chain so far — every number above is clickable to see how it was built

The map of the plan

Each shape is one rep's territory, drawn so workloads come out even. Amber = a watch-item.

showing one region — 24 of 148 territories · color = territory health · click any shape for its checks

Territory 47 — Upper Plains
prescribers worth calling on61
a fair week's work1.18× the average — within range
driving share of the weekabout 38% — heavy
one connected patchyes
The fix: shift two counties to the neighboring territory — or accept lighter coverage here, on the record.

What "workable" means

Five checks, in plain words. Two are rules; three we watch.

Checked first: is a map-based split even right for this market? Yes — prescribing here is spread across thousands of clinicians, not concentrated in a few centers. (If it were concentrated, we'd flag account-based coverage instead.)
⚠
What the spread-out territories cost. In the 3 flagged territories, driving eats about a quarter of planned selling time → roughly 310 prescribers would rarely be seen → about $4.2M of the expected profit wouldn't materialize. The size still works; this is the honest price of the map as drawn.
◐ the dollar figure inherits the borrowed sales-response curve — its confidence is capped, like every number that curve touches.
Re-run at a smaller size Relax the fair-week rule — logged Accept, with this on the record
compared to today's territories
About 34% of prescribers would change hands vs. the current map. Shown for change-planning — it does not affect the verdict, and conversations with affected reps are for managers, not this tool.
what this adds to the leadership view
The story gains its final line: "…and it splits into 148 workable territories (3 to watch)." The verdict travels with the recommendation everywhere it goes.
🔍 Show me how the map was drawn — the splitting method, the seed, and every check's math
← The Recommendation step 8 of 8 — the story is complete Finish: the leadership view →
The recommended field size

132–171 reps works — we recommend 148, and here's why we stand behind it

148 reps
recommended & aligned · the profit-max point inside a 132–171 flat zone
Reach
8,900 / 12,400
prescribers we'll call on (72%)
Revenue
~$214M
3-year, new-patient view
Profit
~$92M
after field-force cost · 3-year, new-patient
Expansion return
~1.4×
profit per $ on added reps
⚠ Note: profit is flat from 132 to 171 reps — 148 is the precise peak; the lower end of the range delivers nearly the same profit for less cost. The size is not the sensitive choice here; the response estimate is.
◐ capped confidence — profit inherits a borrowed sales-response curve

How we got to 148

The full logic, in one line each — open any step to see the math behind it.

1
The market: about 12,400 prescribers write in this class; 8,900 are worth calling on.details ›
12.4k
2
The call plan: targets get about 8 calls a year, weighted to higher-value prescribers.details ›
8/yr
3
Rep capacity: a rep makes about 620 quality calls a year after travel and admin.details ›
620
4
Sales response: each call lifts prescribing, but flattens — estimated from comparable products.details ›
curve
5
The economics: about $1,150 profit per prescription; about $310k fully-loaded per rep.details ›
$1.15k
★
The size: profit is flat from 132–171; 148 is the peak, where the last rep added still pays its way.details ›
148
✓
Reality check: …and it splits into 148 workable territories (3 to watch).details ›
3 to watch

⚠ What we're least sure about

  • Sales response is an estimate, borrowed from comparable products — not this product's own data. Confidence is capped until grounded next cycle.
  • The market is concentrated in a few large accounts — a territory-by-geography deployment may need a second look.
  • All dollar figures are a 3-year, new-patient view.

Where each function landed

Alignment means positions are on the record — not that everyone agreed.

agreedFinanceaffordable within the plan budget.
agreedMarket Accesseligible universe confirmed.
adjustedSalesraised rep capacity to 620/yr from field data.
agreedProducttarget list & tiers confirmed.
flaggedMarketinglow confidence on response — recommends grounding next cycle. Recorded.
agreedSales opsterritories workable, 3 watch-items logged.
